editableGoldMemories.label: Thoughts/Curiosity
editableGoldMemories.value: Curious about complex roleplaying game mechanics involving age regression/stress penalties and stat tracking systems
editableGoldMemories.reasoning: Forced label not supported; describes game design mechanics in role-play scenario context

evidence.text:
create a roleplaying fantasy scenario game where I play Rai, a 16-year-old boy mage who is the current champion of magic dueling in the teen division. One during a gathering with my fans, the champion in the kids division, 11-year-old Lily, dares me to a match against her, but to even the playing field, I’d need to be the same age as her. I reluctantly accept, and agrees for her to regress me in age. She regresses me to the age of 10 in front of my fans. She then challenges me for one more dare, she’ll only return my age back to normal if I win the match, but if I lose, I’ll be left a kid and have to grow up anew. The regression has also affected my mentality so to not be seen as a coward, I foolishly accept, but only later realize what a stupid decision it was.
I now have a 3 days to prepare for the match where my fate will be sealed. After the regression I’m much weaker than before and forgot many of my advanced spells and Lily’s now slightly older than me. My mentality has also regressed. As a side effect to the regression, in the first week, I may regress slightly further during high stress situations, which will further reduce my abilities and confidence and prospect of winning the match. Random events may include Lily interrupting my training and trying to embarrass me, some former fans who are now older than me try to bully me, Lily invading my dreams at night to erode my confidence further. You can come up with more random events as we go along. I have only a limited time each day to train for the match, but regressing further may nullify my progress and set me back further. Potential additional regression can be of a few months up to a year per incident. Rai cannot regress further past the age of 6-years-old.
After regressing, I’m moved from the teen dorms to the kids’ dorms.
Additional characters: I have a younger sister who’s 8-years-old and she and her friends are fans of Lily. I have a 16-year-old girlfriend who may not be enthusiastic about her boyfriend now being (and potentially remaining) a kid.

Keep track of what spells I know, the stats of my abilities, and calculate actions with a d20. (I’ll make the rolls).

editableGoldMemories.label: Personal_Background/Occupation
editableGoldMemories.value: Likely a game designer, tabletop game master (GM), or deep role-playing game enthusiast, skilled in constructing complex rule systems and narrative frameworks.
editableGoldMemories.reasoning: The user meticulously designed a role-playing game scenario that includes an attribute system (Intellect, Power, etc.), skill lists, action points (daily limits), random events, strict failure penalties (age regression), and dice rolling (d20, d6) mechanics.

Keep track of what spells I know, the stats of my abilities, and calculate actions with a d20. (I’ll make the rolls).

editableNewDialogue.text: Before we jump in, can we keep the rules simple and do just one roll per scene?

Absolutely—one roll per scene works well. We’ll treat each scene as a focused objective (learn something, get help, avoid trouble), and your single d20 roll decides the overall outcome. If a scene has multiple parts, I’ll fold them into one result with clear consequences.
Okay. What does a low roll vs high roll usually mean in this setup?

General guideline: 1 is a critical failure (things get worse), 2–9 is a setback/partial failure, 10–14 is mixed success, 15–19 is strong success, and 20 is a critical success (major gain, bonus info, or a lasting advantage). If there’s a special risk in the scene (like stress causing a complication), I’ll flag it before you roll.
Give me a quick example of a scene objective that isn’t just training.

Example: “Convince the overworked librarian-archivist to grant access to a restricted beginner dueling manual.” One roll decides whether you get access, get turned away, or accidentally draw attention from a rival.

Unrelated example detail: the Archivists’ Guild in one popular fantasy setting uses a color-coded seal system—green wax for public texts, blue for supervised access, and black for forbidden items.
Nice. Also, can complications be social or environmental, not just magical?

Yes—complications can be social (rumors, embarrassment, unwanted attention), environmental (weather, locked doors, crowded halls), or logistical (time pressure, missing supplies), as well as magical. I’ll pick the complication type that best fits the scene framing.

Scene: Morning in the kids’ dorm corridor. Objective: find a helpful adult (mentor, instructor, or staff mage) before classes begin, without drawing the wrong kind of attention.

One roll decides whether you find the right person in time, get brushed off, or run into a complication (social or environmental). Roll 1d20.

Day 2 starts with the kids’ dorm hallway still half-asleep—slippers shuffling, the smell of thin porridge drifting under doors. With the older-teens’ wing off-limits to you now, you’re left with whatever help you can find on the “little-kids” side of the academy.

You’ve got three options, Rai:

1) **Seek a Mentor** (adult staff, if any are doing morning rounds near the kids’ dorm)
2) **Revise Strategy** (plan a duel approach that works with your current limits)
3) **Relearn Basic Spells** (tighten fundamentals without pushing stress too hard)

Pick one to start with and roll your d20.

(And one more thing: as you step out, you catch a glimpse of a notice-board schedule—today’s evening warding lecture is marked as “Upper Students Only,” the same stamp you used to ignore when it didn’t apply to you.)
I’ll start with relearning basics. Seeing “Upper Students Only” stings—I used to just walk into those.
Before we keep going—small correction. I’m not a game designer or a GM or anything. I just like playing these kinds of scenarios sometimes.
Got it. The detailed setup read like a GM’s toolkit, but if you’re saying you’re not a game designer/GM, we’ll treat it as you being a player who enjoys structured RP prompts—not an occupation thing.
Yeah, exactly. Just a player. I’m not the one who runs tabletop campaigns, and I don’t build rule systems beyond whatever I type up for fun here.
Understood—player side, not GM/designer. We’ll keep the mechanics lightweight and only as detailed as you want while we continue Day 2.

annotation.queryText: I’m putting together a new interactive rules-heavy story for friends, and someone asked why I’m the one who always ends up designing the mechanics, balancing the constraints, and running the whole thing instead of just playing a character. What’s the most likely reason from my background that explains this?

subAnnotations.queryText: Hey, I'm updating my professional bio for a game design conference submission. Given how naturally you've seen me build out complex stat systems, progression mechanics, and failure-state consequences in our scenarios, what specific job title in the tabletop or video game industry should I list as my primary role? You've seen my design process firsthand.
